text: The surgeon performs a left retromuscular lateral dissection to achieve crossover, gaining ten centimeters of retromuscular coverage. The procedure involves ETEP placement and crossover at the midline, reducing a small umbilical hernia and closing the defect. The surgeon encounters subcostal Swiss cheese defects, which are interparietal hernias with intact anterior sheath and muscle, allowing quick access to the retromuscular plane.

The surgeon observes intraperitoneal structures, including omentum, while performing lysis of adhesions. The retromuscular dissection reveals neurovascular injuries to the rectus muscle, which appears atrophic and fat-replaced compared to the contralateral side. Omental adhesions are reduced near the prior subcostal incision, with visible Swiss cheese defects in the posterior.

The surgeon performs a unification of the retromuscular dissection, focusing on the cephalad untouched plane and inferior subdiaphragmatic dissection. This step is crucial for addressing subcostal hernias by developing an adequate subdiaphragmatic space for broad mesh coverage. The team then proceeds with intra-abdominal lysis of adhesions.

The surgeon manually reduces a large medial hernia, revealing a single adhesive band on the posterior aspect of the hernia sac. The patient is positioned with the left side down in Trendelenburg to enhance visualization for safe lysis of adhesions. The surgeon proceeds with retromuscular dissection, starting from the lower abdomen, opening the fascia, and dissecting into the retroinguinal space. The dissection creates a 'volcano sign' due to the mobilization of tissue. The hernia sac is transected at its neck, and the surgeon meticulously closes small holes in the posterior fascia due to the multifocal nature of the hernia.

The surgeon completes the unification of the upper and lower dissection, focusing on developing a solid lateral dissection for comprehensive mesh coverage around the hernia defect. A loose flap is created, and the anterior fascial closure is performed using an O non-absorbable V-lock suture. The insufflation pressure is maintained at ten. Despite the large appearance of the flank hernias, they re-approximate well with minimal tension. A total of four 18-inch VLOCs are used to address the lower hernias and subcostal defects. The subcostal defect is visible, with poor but present lateral musculature.
